_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Developmental abnormalities (abdominal wall defects) in the region of the umbilicus.
Diagnosed on antenatal ultrasonography or present at birth as neonatal emergencies, requiring urgent treatment.
Associated with exposed bowel on the abdominal wall.
A careful physical exam at birth revealing abdominal contents external to the abdominal wall is diagnostic.

ppd_preauth.q: Did the physical exam lead to clinical diagnosis of Gastroschisis/Exomphalos at birth?
Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and line of treatment?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Physical exam diagnosis of Gastroschisis/Exomphalos at birth not documented.
Clinical photographs supporting diagnosis not submitted at pre-authorization.
Clinical notes lack detailed history, signs & symptoms, or indication for procedure.
Detailed operative notes or procedure details are missing at claim submission.
Post-operative photographs not submitted at claim submission.
Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) lacking daily vitals or line of treatment.

additional_information.special_empanelment: Care at Tertiary Center equipped to handle the resuscitative and surgical needs of the newborn
clinical_key_pointers: Exomphalos (Omphalocele) and Gastroschisis are developmental abnormalities (abdominal wall defects) in the region of the umbilicus, diagnosed on antenatal ultrasonography or present at birth as neonatal emergencies, requiring urgent treatment.
Gastroschisis and omphalocele are associated with exposed bowel on the abdominal wall.
Omphaloceles are abdominal wall defects ranging from 4 to 12 cm in size and can be located centrally, or in the epigastric or hypogastric regions.

This congenital hernia into the base of the umbilical cord is caused by incomplete folding of the embryonic disc and failure of the umbilical ring to form normally. The hernia is covered by fused amniotic membrane and peritoneum.

The size of the defect in the abdominal wall and the volume of the sac are variable: An intact sac is shiny and translucent but lacks a blood supply and begins to dry out and deteriorate after birth. Within 12 h it becomes opaque and yellowish; later, it becomes black, inelastic and desiccated.
Omphalocele is associated with extraintestinal problems, such as genetic trisomies, hypoglycemia (Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome), and multiorgan defects.
Exomphalos major: Defect is more than 5 cm in diameter; Cord is attached to the base of sac; It may contain liver, stomach, etc.; Primary closure may not be possible.
Exomphalos minor: Defect is less than 5 cm in diameter; Cord is attached to the tip of sac; It contains only bowel; Primary repair is easy.
During the neonatal period, infants with omphalocele and gastroschisis do not routinely receive any further investigations unless there are signs of dysmorphia.
No intrauterine interventions are available for the treatment of omphalocele or gastroschisis.
surgical_options: Surgical repair of omphalocele is elective for defects without rupture of the membranous sac.
If membranous sac is ruptured, immediate intervention is required.
For smaller defects with ruptured sac, attempt primary closure under general anesthesia (GA).
If primary closure is not possible (for larger defects or those with extensive herniation), perform staged closure or silo method.
additional_information.pre_op_preparation: Therapy is undertaken after delivery of the infant, with goals of temperature maintenance, fluid resuscitation, and preventing additional fluid loss from the abdominal contents with appropriate care of the herniated viscera, paying particular attention to the preservation of its blood supply.
additional_information.post_op_care: Ventral hernia develops at weak spot post repair, which requires surgical repair after about six months.
additional_information.referral_criteria: Referral of cases to the appropriate level of care (as required) for treatment of patients under PMJAY and selection of corresponding Health Benefit Package.
